summary: Cthulhu Life World defaults to slow-burn danger, but trap and danger frequency dynamically adjust through truth exposure, corruption or assimilation, occult contact, social normalcy, publicity level, player actions, and difficulty pressure.

baseDangerCurve.guidance: Danger should mostly appear as atmosphere, family unease, odd coincidences, dreams, or hidden attention unless talents, exposure, or family background justify more.
The world may introduce rumors, warnings, strange NPC behavior, or small tests, but should not force full horror routes.
Exploration, friendships, school, online rumors, family secrets, and early occult contact can raise risk.
Danger depends strongly on the player character's route, knowledge, relationships, faction ties, and accumulated exposure.
If truth exposure, corruption, occult contact, or world escalation are high, traps and irreversible choices can become frequent.

dynamicDrivers.effect: Higher truth exposure increases accurate clues, dangerous attention, and routes where knowledge itself becomes a risk.
Higher corruption increases power temptation, body or mind changes, entity attention, social abnormality, and irreversible bargains.
Higher occult contact increases ritual hooks, cult access, forbidden books, dream invitations, and trap density.
Lower social normalcy increases isolation, official suspicion, cult approach, family breakdown, and difficulty returning to ordinary life.
Higher public abnormality levels increase open danger, official lockdowns, faction conflict, and world-scale consequences.
Repeated exploration, forbidden questions, risky rituals, and suspicious NPC contact increase event and trap frequency.
Family background, factions, allies, status, and luck can reduce or redirect danger, but may also create higher-level enemies.

trapFrequencyBands.description: Rare traps. Mostly atmosphere and foreshadowing.
Occasional misleading clues or mild consequences.
Some choices carry hidden costs; exploration can attract attention.
Traps, bait, and dangerous opportunities appear regularly when the player engages hidden systems.
Most hidden-world interactions carry serious risk or attention.
The world is actively hostile; traps, pursuit, corruption, and irreversible outcomes are frequent.

aiRules: Default to slow-burn danger, not immediate constant punishment.
Increase trap and danger frequency when the player character pursues truth, uses occult methods, accumulates corruption, loses social normalcy, or repeatedly acts recklessly.
Decrease or redirect danger when the player character avoids the hidden world, has strong protection, maintains social normalcy, or makes cautious choices.
High Luck can create escapes and helpful coincidences, but it can also pull the player character toward rare and dangerous opportunities.
Traps must be narratively justified by world logic, NPC motives, faction interests, entity attention, or player behavior.
The AI may hide exact mechanics, but should provide fuzzy warning labels when the player character can reasonably sense risk.
